sdo:itemDesc.dc:title: Handwritten letter from Brüll to Schenker, dated February 26, [1901]
sdo:itemDesc.dc:description: Believing that Schenker merits a place on the contributor panel of Universal Edition, Ignaz Brüll encourages him to speak to Josef Weinberger.

tei:opener.tei:salute: Verehrter Herr Doktor!The editorial dating of this letter is based on the correspondence between Julius Röntgen and Schenker, and the founding dates of Universal Edition: February 6, 1901, Ministry of the Interior agreement to establishment of company; June 15, first general meeting of company; August 9, announcement of company’s establishment.

tei:div.tei:p: Ich habe schon vor längerer Zeit Herrn Weinberger von Ihnen gesprochen, ihm gesagt, daß Sie, ein so eminenter Musiker und gewissenhafter Mann, eine höchst vortreffliche Acquisition für die Mitarbeiterschaft an der „Edition“ sein würden. Er antwortete mir nichts weiter, als daß nicht er persönlich, sondern das ganze Comité in den Sitzungen die Mitarbeiter bestimmt hat.This remark suggests that there may have been resistance to Schenker among the UE editorial committee. — By March 15, 1901, Schenker was himself representing the committee in an approach to Julius Röntgen (NMI C 176-02) with a view to the latter’s becoming a contributor. Röntgen replied three days later. Clearly Schenker had by then been enlisted to the contributor panel, the result being his own edition of selected Keyboard Works of C. P. E. Bach, published in 1902.

Die „ganze Erde“ dürfte aber doch noch nicht vertheilt sein u. ich meine, daß W’s Stimme im Comité den Einfluß ist. Und da er Sie ersuchen ließ, zu ihm zu kommen, so fände ich nichts dabei – vielmehr ich fände es ganz richtig, wenn Sie ihn besuchen. Verzeihen Sie, daß ich diese meine Ansicht ausdrücke – ich bin ja nicht berechtigt zu rathen – betrachten Sie diese Freiheit als Ausfluß meiner wahrhaft freundschaflichen
Gesinnung
Ich freue mich, bald Sie u. Ihre nova zu sehen.

I spoke about you long ago to Mr. Weinberger, saying that you, as so eminent a musician and so scrupulous a man, would be a most excellent addition to the contributor panel of the Universal Edition. All that he would say to me was that it is not he personally but the entire committee in its meetings that has appointed the contributors.

The “whole world” however ought surely not still to be at loggerheads, and in my view it is Weinberger’s voice that carries weight in the committee. And since he allowed that you might approach him, I see no harm in it – indeed, I would think it entirely right if you were to pay him a visit. Forgive me for expressing my opinion – I am certainly not authorized to offer advice – so consider this liberty evidence of my truly friendly
disposition.
I look forward to seeing you and your latest works soon.
